danger_signals.visual_form: Animated enemy doll sprites moving toward player character
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate threat; touching enemy causes instant loss of life

danger_signals.visual_form: Dark or obscured level layouts requiring player anticipation
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard; players must predict tile and enemy positions in advance
Impassable obstacle; cannot be flipped; enemy collision point

danger_signals.visual_form: Graffiti-patterned floor tiles in House of Vinci
danger_signals.meaning: Restricted/dangerous zone; unflippable tiles that can create stalemate conditions

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy doll variant with same flip ability as player
danger_signals.meaning: Advanced threat; enemy can manipulate terrain unpredictably

safety_signals.visual_form: Floor tile with kanji 五 (go, meaning 'five') or time symbol
safety_signals.meaning: Beneficial collectible; grants five extra seconds on timer

safety_signals.visual_form: Layered tiles revealed through repeated flipping
safety_signals.meaning: Progression mechanic; hidden patterns provide strategic advantage
Safe interaction point; allows player to slam into enemies without taking damage

safety_signals.visual_form: Solid wall perimeter around 5x7 grid room
safety_signals.meaning: Containment zone; safe barrier; enemies destroyed by flipping into walls

safety_signals.visual_form: Star counter displayed on screen (tracked separately per player)
safety_signals.meaning: Progress metric; visual feedback of level completion status

faction_indicators.color_identity: Blue and green colored boys in vests and caps
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinguishable by primary color (blue player 1, green player 2)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Players occupy floor tiles; tracked separately on screen with individual star/life counters

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Doll sprite design varying by world/palace
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Each palace contains different enemy doll types with unique behaviors (walking, swimming, flipping)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Level select screen displays each palace with occupying enemy doll types

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Artist-themed enemy variant; draws pictures as attack/environmental modification
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Regional difference: draws woman's face normally; under time pressure draws nude torso (JP) or sailor uniform (US)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: House of Vinci palace; creates graffiti tiles that modify terrain

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: One boss per world; defeated to advance narrative
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Each world completion scene shows girlfriend being transported to next palace location

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy dolls visible on playfield grid; star counter below maximum
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy dolls continue wandering and attempting to collide with player; player can flip tiles to propel enemies

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Empty playfield; full star counter; transition scene plays
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progression to next level; narrative cutscene showing girlfriend relocation to next palace

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Remaining enemies on board with all flippable tiles exhausted; bolted metal or graffiti tiles dominate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Level completion without destroying all dolls; occurs when terrain becomes unflippable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player life counter decrements on screen; character sprite vanishes or transitions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: If co-op: surviving player continues solo; if solo: level restart or game over

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Timer visibly depleting; some worlds (House of Vinci) show enemy behavior modification (Artist draws different picture)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy behavioral shifts; altered tile drawing patterns; increased urgency visual feedback

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Limited visual range; hidden or dimmed tile/enemy positions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must rely on memory and anticipation; blind positioning of flips

environmental_storytelling.example: Level select screen displays palace locations; cutscenes show girlfriend being transported deeper into realm with each world completion, creating spatial/narrative progression
Multiple hidden tile patterns under single floor position communicate progression through repeated interaction; reveals reward or obstacle through visual layer discovery
Each palace displays its unique enemy doll roster on level select screen; House of Vinci identified by Artist dolls; communicates world identity through enemy visual design
Each of ten levels culminates in boss encounter; defeat triggers cutscene showing girlfriend relocation; boss serves as visual punctuation to world narrative

environmental_storytelling.technique: Regional visual censorship as narrative shift
environmental_storytelling.example: Artist doll drawing changes from nude (JP) to clothed sailor uniform (US) under time pressure; same mechanical trigger, different cultural narrative
5x7 floor tile grid surrounded by solid wall perimeter; communicates single-room contained challenge; wall serves both as narrative container and gameplay mechanic

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Wanders room attempting to collide with player; basic locomotion pattern
Modified movement pattern simulating aquatic motion; alternative threat approach
Flips random tiles same as player; unpredictably modifies terrain; higher threat level than standard dolls
Draws woman's face on graffiti tiles; creates unflippable terrain modification
Draws altered picture (nude torso/sailor uniform depending on region); signals desperation/theme shift
Enhanced variant of standard doll; serves as culminating encounter; defeat unlocks world progression
Stands on floor tiles; initiates tile flips; interacts with adjacent tiles; moves on grid
Instant life loss; character sprite removal; immediate consequence

notes: Mendel Palace uses minimal but explicit visual language: color-coded players (blue/green), doll sprite variation per world, tile pattern layering, and numeric HUD feedback (lives/stars). Danger is communicated through proximity (enemies) and terrain type (bolted/graffiti tiles). Safety through collectibles (timer extension) and game mechanics (spinner tiles). The game's visual communication is primarily diegetic—enemy presence is constant visible threat, tile accessibility is visual, and world progression is communicated through cutscenes and enemy roster display. Regional differences (artist doll drawing content) show how same mechanical trigger carries cultural narrative weight. The game prioritizes clarity of threat/safety through sprite design and immediate visual feedback rather than complex environmental storytelling.
sources_used: tcrf (Cutting Room Floor - Mendel Palace article)
